question_types.label: Projectile motion components from graphs
question_types.notes: Identifying horizontal (constant) and vertical (linearly varying) velocity components of a projectile from time graphs.

stem_header_content.caption: Parabolic path of struck ball
stem_header_content.alt: Diagram of a ball following a parabolic path with a dashed arc, dot at left ground position.

stem_header_content.value: A ball is struck at time t = 0 and follows the parabolic path shown in the diagram above. The following graphs show quantities possibly associated with the motion as a function of time t. Assume that air resistance is negligible and that the positive directions are upward and to the right.

stem_header_content.caption: Five candidate graphs (A)–(E)
stem_header_content.alt: Five graphs of a quantity vs t labeled (A) inverted-V triangle, (B) V shape, (C) line with positive slope starting below O, (D) line with negative slope starting above O, (E) horizontal nonzero line.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: Which graph represents the horizontal component of the velocity of the ball?

(A) Graph A
(B) Graph B
(C) Graph C
(D) Graph D
(E) Graph E

ordered_content.value: Which graph represents the vertical component of the velocity of the ball?

(A) Graph A
(B) Graph B

(D) Graph D

question_types.label: Constant-acceleration kinematics on a frictionless incline
question_types.notes: Recognizing that an object on a frictionless incline experiences the same constant acceleration component along the incline regardless of direction of motion.

options: (A) It has a constant acceleration while moving up the plane and a greater acceleration when moving down the plane.
(B) It has a constant acceleration while moving up the plane and a smaller acceleration when moving down the plane.
(C) It moves with a constant velocity both up and down the plane.
(D) It has the same acceleration as it moves up and down the plane.
(E) It has a continually varying acceleration as it moves up and down the plane.
questions.correct_option: (D) It has the same acceleration as it moves up and down the plane.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.caption: Object on inclined plane
ordered_content.alt: Right triangle inclined plane with an object at the bottom moving up with initial velocity v0 along the incline.

ordered_content.value: An object with initial velocity v_0, as shown above, slides up and then down a long, frictionless, inclined plane. Which of the following is true of the object as it moves?

(A) It has a constant acceleration while moving up the plane and a greater acceleration when moving down the plane.
(B) It has a constant acceleration while moving up the plane and a smaller acceleration when moving down the plane.
(C) It moves with a constant velocity both up and down the plane.
(D) It has the same acceleration as it moves up and down the plane.
(E) It has a continually varying acceleration as it moves up and down the plane.

question_types.label: Free-fall distance scaling with time
question_types.notes: Both questions require applying the d = (1/2)gt^2 kinematic relation to compare distances at different free-fall durations.

options: (E) It cannot be determined from the information given.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: Two objects are dropped from rest from the same height. Object A falls through a distance d_A during a time t, and object B falls through a distance d_B during a time 2t. If air resistance is negligible, what is the relationship between d_A and d_B ?

(A) d_A = (1/4) d_B
(B) d_A = (1/2) d_B
(C) d_A = 2 d_B
(D) d_A = 4 d_B
(E) It cannot be determined from the information given.

questions.notes: Combines kinematics of dropped ball (h = (1/2)g t^2) and launched ball (y = v_0 t - (1/2) g t^2) to find meeting time.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.caption: Tower with two balls
ordered_content.alt: A tower of height h with ball 1 at the top dropped from rest and ball 2 launched upward from the ground with speed v0.

ordered_content.value: Ball 1 is dropped from rest at time t = 0 from a tower of height h, as shown above. At the same instant, ball 2 is launched upward from the ground with initial speed v_0. If air resistance is negligible, at what time t will the two balls pass each other?

question_types.label: Reading constant acceleration regions on a v-t graph
question_types.notes: Identifying intervals on a velocity-vs-time graph where the slope (acceleration) is constant and nonzero.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.caption: Velocity vs time graph with segments PQ, QR, RS, ST, TU
ordered_content.alt: A piecewise velocity-vs-time graph: horizontal PQ at top, sloped down QR to point R near 0, sloped further down RS to minimum S below 0, sloped up ST to T at 0, horizontal TU at 0.

ordered_content.value: The graph above shows velocity as a function of time for an object moving along a straight line. For which of the following sections of the graph is the acceleration constant and nonzero?

(A) QR only
(B) ST only
(C) PQ and TU only
(D) RS and ST only
(E) PQ, RS, ST, and TU

question_types.label: Differentiation of polynomial v(t) to find instantaneous acceleration
question_types.notes: Take the time-derivative of v(t) = 3.0 t^2 - 2.0 t + 4.0 and evaluate at t = 2.0 s.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: The velocity of a particle moving along the x-axis is given as a function of time by the expression v(t) = 3.0 t^2 - 2.0 t + 4.0, where v is in meters per second and t is in seconds. What is the acceleration of the particle at t = 2.0 s?

question_types.label: Parametric circular motion: radius and constant speed
question_types.notes: Given x = 5 cos(3t), y = 5 sin(3t): identify radius and constant tangential speed v = r*omega.

stem_header_content.value:
A particle moves in a circle in such a way that the x- and y-coordinates of its motion, given in meters as functions of time t in seconds, are:

x = 5 cos(3t)
y = 5 sin(3t).

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: What is the radius of the circle?

options: (A) It is always equal to 5 m/s.
(B) It is always equal to 15 m/s.
(C) It oscillates with a range of 0 to 5 m/s.
(D) It oscillates with a range of 0 to 15 m/s.
(E) It oscillates with a range of 5 to 15 m/s.
children.correct_option: (B) It is always equal to 15 m/s.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: Which of the following is true of the speed of the particle?

(A) It is always equal to 5 m/s.
(B) It is always equal to 15 m/s.
(C) It oscillates with a range of 0 to 5 m/s.
(D) It oscillates with a range of 0 to 15 m/s.
(E) It oscillates with a range of 5 to 15 m/s.

question_types.label: Atwood-style pulley with massive wheel — comparing tensions
question_types.notes: Massive accelerating pulley implies unequal tensions: T2 > T1 to provide the net torque on the wheel.

options: (A) T_1 = T_2 because the wheel has mass.
(B) T_1 = T_2 because both blocks have the same acceleration.
(C) T_1 > T_2 because m_1 is farther from the wheel than m_2.
(D) T_1 > T_2 because m_1 accelerates upward.
(E) T_2 > T_1 because an unbalanced clockwise torque is needed to accelerate the wheel clockwise.
questions.correct_option: (E) T_2 > T_1 because an unbalanced clockwise torque is needed to accelerate the wheel clockwise.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.caption: Pulley with two hanging masses
ordered_content.alt: A wheel of mass m with string passing over it; tension T1 to a hanging mass m1 on the left lower position and tension T2 to a hanging mass m2 on the right at higher position.

ordered_content.value: Two blocks of masses m_1 and m_2 are connected by a massless string that passes over a wheel of mass m, as shown above. The string does not slip on the wheel and exerts forces T_1 and T_2 on the blocks. When the wheel is released from rest in the position shown, it undergoes an angular acceleration and rotates clockwise. Which of the following statements about T_1 and T_2 is correct?

(A) T_1 = T_2 because the wheel has mass.
(B) T_1 = T_2 because both blocks have the same acceleration.
(C) T_1 > T_2 because m_1 is farther from the wheel than m_2.
(D) T_1 > T_2 because m_1 accelerates upward.
(E) T_2 > T_1 because an unbalanced clockwise torque is needed to accelerate the wheel clockwise.

question_types.label: Center of mass of point particles on a number line
question_types.notes: Direct application of x_cm = sum(m_i x_i) / sum(m_i).

question_types.label: Velocity from a time-varying net force F = bt
question_types.notes: Integrate F = bt to obtain impulse, then v = impulse/m = b t^2 / (2m).

questions.notes: Could fit Unit 4 (impulse) too; categorized under dynamics for chain F→a→v.

(A) bt/m
(B) bt^2/(2m)
(C) bt^2/m
(D) b*sqrt(t)/m
(E) b/(mt)

question_types.notes: Apply F_net = (m_total) a to find required force on a system of three blocks.

question_types.label: Kinetic friction with an angled applied force
question_types.notes: Resolve angled force into components, find normal force from vertical equilibrium, then apply f = mu_k * N.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.caption: Block pulled by angled force
ordered_content.alt: A block of mass m on a horizontal rough surface with force F applied at angle phi above the horizontal.

ordered_content.value: A block of mass m is pulled across a rough surface, as shown above. The coefficient of friction between the block and the surface is mu_k. The force F that pulls the block is exerted at the angle phi shown. Which of the following is an expression for the magnitude of the frictional force on the block if it is moving at constant speed?

(A) mu_k m g
(B) mu_k F cos(phi)
(C) mu_k (F sin(phi) + m g)
(D) mu_k (F sin(phi) - m g)
(E) mu_k (m g - F sin(phi))

question_types.label: Kepler / orbital ratios for an elliptical comet orbit
question_types.notes: All three parts work the same elliptical-orbit stimulus: KE/speed comparison via energy + angular momentum conservation, and force ratio via inverse-square gravity.

stem_header_content.caption: Elliptical comet orbit around the Sun (not to scale)
stem_header_content.alt: An elliptical orbit with the Sun marked inside; position 2 on the left at the farthest point, position 1 on the right at the nearest point. Note: figure not drawn to scale.

stem_header_content.value: The elliptical orbit of a comet is shown above. Positions 1 and 2 are, respectively, the farthest and nearest positions to the Sun, and at position 1 the distance from the comet to the Sun is 10 times that at position 2.

options: (C) at its maximum value for the orbit
(D) at its minimum value for the orbit, but greater than zero
children.correct_option: (C) at its maximum value for the orbit

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: At position 2, the comet's kinetic energy is

(A) the same as at position 1
(B) less than at position 1
(C) at its maximum value for the orbit
(D) at its minimum value for the orbit, but greater than zero
(E) equal to zero

children.notes: Wait — printed key for Q22 is C; problem says position 2 is FARTHEST so KE there is minimum. The published key reads C; some references list D. We use printed key C.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: What is the ratio v_1 / v_2 of the speed of the comet at position 1 to the speed at position 2?

children.notes: F ~ 1/r^2 with r1 = r2/10 → F1/F2 = 100. The printed answer key gives A (1/100). Either there is a key/orientation inconsistency in the released exam OR the labeling of positions in different printings differs. We follow the printed CB key.

question_types.label: Units of momentum (dimensional analysis)
question_types.notes: Recognize kg·m/s = N·s as a unit of momentum (and impulse).

question_types.label: Conservation laws in a glancing collision
question_types.notes: Momentum is always conserved during a collision; KE may or may not be conserved depending on whether collision is elastic.

options: (A) Momentum is always conserved, and kinetic energy may be conserved.
(B) Kinetic energy is always conserved, and momentum may be conserved.
(C) Momentum is always conserved, and kinetic energy is never conserved.
(D) Both momentum and kinetic energy are always conserved.
(E) Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved.
questions.correct_option: (A) Momentum is always conserved, and kinetic energy may be conserved.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: An object moving on a horizontal, frictionless surface makes a glancing collision with another object initially at rest on the surface. In this case which of the following is true about momentum and kinetic energy?

(A) Momentum is always conserved, and kinetic energy may be conserved.
(B) Kinetic energy is always conserved, and momentum may be conserved.
(C) Momentum is always conserved, and kinetic energy is never conserved.
(D) Both momentum and kinetic energy are always conserved.
(E) Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved.

question_types.label: Equal-impulse comparison of momentum changes for unequal masses
question_types.notes: Impulse-momentum theorem: same net force * same time → identical change in momentum, independent of mass.

options: (A) It is smaller than the change in linear momentum of the larger mass but not zero.
(B) It is larger than the change in linear momentum of the larger mass.
(C) It is equal to the change in linear momentum of the larger mass.
(E) It depends on the relative diameters of the two masses.
questions.correct_option: (C) It is equal to the change in linear momentum of the larger mass.

ordered_content.value: Identical net forces act for the same length of time on two different spherical masses. Which of the following describes the change in linear momentum of the smaller mass compared to that of the larger mass?

(A) It is smaller than the change in linear momentum of the larger mass but not zero.
(B) It is larger than the change in linear momentum of the larger mass.
(C) It is equal to the change in linear momentum of the larger mass.
(D) It is zero.
(E) It depends on the relative diameters of the two masses.

question_types.label: Reading SHM y(t) graph for time of max upward velocity and spring constant
question_types.notes: Identify the moment when y(t) crosses equilibrium moving upward, and use period to back out spring constant.

stem_header_content.caption: Vertical position y vs time for a vertical spring oscillator
stem_header_content.alt: Graph of y in meters vs t in seconds showing a sinusoidal oscillation with peaks near y = -0.10 m and troughs near y = -0.35 m, period about 0.7 s.

stem_header_content.value: An unstretched ideal spring hangs vertically from a fixed support. A 0.4 kg object is then attached to the lower end of the spring. The object is pulled down to a distance of 0.35 m below the unstretched position and released from rest at time t = 0. A graph of the subsequent vertical position y of the lower end of the spring as a function of t is given above, where y = 0 when the spring was initially unstretched.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: At which of the following times is the upward velocity of the object the greatest?

children.notes: From the equilibrium position (midpoint between peak and trough at y = -0.225 m) and m = 0.4 kg, k = m g / x_eq ~ 16 N/m.

question_types.label: Acceleration vs displacement graph for SHM
question_types.notes: For SHM, a = -omega^2 x, so a(x) is a straight line through the origin with negative slope.

questions.notes: Printed key gives A, but a = -omega^2 x is a line of negative slope through origin (graph A in this layout where line slopes down from top-left to bottom-right).
options: (A) Graph A — line of negative slope through origin
(B) Graph B — line of positive slope through origin
(C) Graph C — V-shape opening upward at the origin
(D) Graph D — concave-up parabola with minimum at the origin
(E) Graph E — concave-down parabola with maximum above the origin
questions.correct_option: (A) Graph A — line of negative slope through origin

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.caption: Five candidate a-vs-x graphs labeled (A)–(E)
ordered_content.alt: Five candidate graphs of acceleration a vs displacement x: (A) line with negative slope through origin, (B) line with positive slope through origin, (C) V-shape with vertex at origin, (D) concave-up parabola with minimum at origin, (E) concave-down parabola with maximum at origin.

ordered_content.value: An object undergoes simple harmonic motion along the x-axis shown above, where x = 0 is the object's equilibrium position. Which of the following graphs best shows the relationship between the object's acceleration a and its displacement x from equilibrium? (Assume positive a to be acceleration directed to the right.)

(A) Graph A — line of negative slope through origin
(B) Graph B — line of positive slope through origin
(C) Graph C — V-shape opening upward at the origin
(D) Graph D — concave-up parabola with minimum at the origin
(E) Graph E — concave-down parabola with maximum above the origin

question_types.label: SHM spring-mass: deriving v(t), x(t), k, free-body diagrams, and damped v-t sketch
question_types.notes: Multipart FRQ asks for sinusoidal v(t) and x(t) from a v-t graph, k from period, FBDs in two motion directions on a rough surface, and a damped sketch.

stem_header_content.caption: Spring-mass on frictionless table (Experiment 1)
stem_header_content.alt: A horizontal spring of constant k attached to a fixed wall on the left and a 0.30 kg block on the right; the block sits on a frictionless table at equilibrium position x = 0.

stem_header_content.caption: Velocity v vs time t for Experiment 1
stem_header_content.alt: Graph of velocity v (m/s) from -0.2 to 0.2 vs time t (s) from 0.0 to 2.0 showing sinusoidal oscillation with amplitude about 0.16 m/s and period roughly 0.7 s, starting at 0 with negative slope.

stem_header_content.value:
Mech. 1.
Experiment 1. A block of mass 0.30 kg is placed on a frictionless table and is attached to one end of a horizontal spring of spring constant k, as shown above. The other end of the spring is attached to a fixed wall. The block is set into oscillatory motion by stretching the spring and releasing the block from rest at time t = 0. A motion detector is used to record the position of the block as it oscillates. The resulting graph of velocity v versus time t is shown below. The positive direction for all quantities is to the right.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: (a) Determine the equation for v(t), including numerical values for all constants.

ordered_content.value: (b) Given that the equilibrium position is at x = 0, determine the equation for x(t), including numerical values for all constants.

ordered_content.value: (c) Calculate the value of k.

ordered_content.value:
Experiment 2. The block and spring arrangement is now placed on a rough surface, as shown below. The block is displaced so that the spring is compressed a distance d and released from rest.

(d) On the dots below that represent the block, draw and label the forces (not components) that act on the block when the spring is compressed a distance x = d/2 and the block is moving in the direction indicated below each dot. (One dot is labeled 'Toward the equilibrium position', the other 'Away from the equilibrium position'.)

question_types.label: Designing a conservation-of-mechanical-energy lab using gravitational PE → KE
question_types.notes: Multipart FRQ asks the student to plan equipment, procedure, calculations, and explain energy gain/loss in a lab where gravitational PE converts to translational KE.

stem_header_content.value:
Mech. 2.
You are to perform an experiment investigating the conservation of mechanical energy involving a transformation from initial gravitational potential energy to translational kinetic energy.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value:
(a) You are given the equipment listed below, all the supports required to hold the equipment, and a lab table. On the list below, indicate each piece of equipment you would use by checking the line next to each item.

____ Track       ____ Meterstick       ____ Set of objects of different masses
____ Cart        ____ Electronic balance ____ Lightweight low-friction pulley
____ String      ____ Stopwatch

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: (b) Outline a procedure for performing the experiment. Include a diagram of your experimental setup. Label the equipment in your diagram. Also include a description of the measurements you would make and a symbol for each measurement.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: (c) Give a detailed account of the calculations of gravitational potential energy and translational kinetic energy both before and after the transformation, in terms of the quantities measured in part (b).

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: (d) After your first trial, your calculations show that the energy increased during the experiment. Assuming you made no mathematical errors, give a reasonable explanation for this result.

source_reference.worksheet_name: CB AP Physics C: Mechanics Released Exam 2012 — Section I (MCQ) + Section II (FRQ) — extracted 2026-05

ordered_content.value: (e) On all other trials, your calculations show that the energy decreased during the experiment. Assuming you made no mathematical errors, give a reasonable physical explanation for the fact that the average energy you determined decreased. Include references to conservative and nonconservative forces, as appropriate.
